title: Roast Pork Loin with Crackling
subtitle: Scored skin, dried overnight, hot oven start for shattering crackling
excerpt: A proper crackling starts with dry skin. Score the rind deeply, rub with fine salt, and leave uncovered in the fridge overnight. The hot oven start blisters the rind before the meat cooks through; the low phase finishes the interior. The result is crackling that shatters rather than bends, surrounding a joint that is just pink through the centre.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: loin of pork roasting and crackling guidance. Delia Smith, Complete Cookery Course (1978): the overnight drying and salt-rubbing method for crackling. Traditional British Sunday roast practice.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The overnight drying step is essential for crackling. Score and salt the rind the night before and refrigerate uncovered on a rack. Do not cover with cling film: the skin must remain dry.
recipe.temperatureNote: initial blast, then 180°C for the remainder

glossaryTerms.definition: The crisp, blistered rind of a roast pork joint, formed when the scored and salted skin is exposed to very high heat, which drives out moisture and causes the fat beneath to puff and blister.

content.text: is not an accident. It requires dry skin, deep scoring, fine salt, and a hot oven. Miss any one of these and you get soft, leathery rind instead of something that shatters at the table. The overnight drying step does most of the work while you sleep; the rest is simply sequencing the oven temperature.

A rolled loin joint of 1.5 kg takes about 80 to 90 minutes total: 25 minutes at 240°C for the crackling, then 55 to 65 minutes at 180°C to bring the interior to 70°C. Pork loin should not be served pink; 70°C to 72°C at the centre gives a just-cooked result without drying the meat out.

items.prepNote: for the rind (fine salt, not flakes)

content.text: A roasting rack inside the roasting pan is important: it lifts the joint so air circulates beneath the rind and the rendered fat drains away from the skin. Skin that sits in fat turns soft.

Using a sharp knife or the tip of a craft knife, score the rind in lines 1 cm apart, cutting through the skin and fat down to the meat. The scores allow salt to penetrate and give the skin room to blister. Rub {{sea-salt-fine}} of fine salt thoroughly into the rind, pushing it into each score. Pat {{sea-salt-flakes}} and {{black-pepper}} onto the meat surfaces. Set the joint rind-side up on a rack over a tray. Refrigerate uncovered overnight.

Take the joint from the fridge 1 hour before cooking. Pat the rind dry with kitchen paper if any moisture has surfaced. Rub the rind with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil. If using fennel seeds, press them into the scores now. Heat the oven to 240°C (220°C fan). Roast on the rack for 25 minutes until the rind is blistered, puffed, and turning golden.

Drop the oven to 180°C (160°C fan) without opening the door any longer than necessary. Continue roasting for 55 to 65 minutes until the thickest part of the meat reads 70°C to 72°C. Rest for 20 minutes under loose foil before carving. Do not cover the crackling tightly: trapped steam softens it.

Pour off the fat from the roasting pan. Set the pan on the hob over medium heat. Add {{dry-cider}} of cider and scrape up the sediment. Add {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock and reduce by half. Season and strain into a jug.

rows.symptom: Crackling soft and leathery rather than blistered
rows.cause: Skin was not dry enough before roasting, or the oven was not fully preheated to 240°C
rows.fix: Give the oven a full 20 minutes to reach temperature. Pat the rind completely dry. If already in the oven, raise to 240°C and roast for a further 10 minutes.

rows.symptom: Crackling burning before meat is cooked
rows.cause: Blast phase too long, or oven runs very hot
rows.fix: Cover the rind with a sheet of foil for the final 30 minutes of the lower-heat phase.
Pull at 70°C and rest immediately. A probe is the only reliable guard against overcooking lean loin.

rows.symptom: Scores closed up during roasting and rind did not blister evenly
rows.cause: Scores not deep enough or too narrow
rows.fix: Score to at least 3 mm depth and 8 to 10 mm wide. Shallow scores close under heat.

content.text: The roast pork loin with crackling is a staple of the British Sunday table from November through March, when roast lamb gives way to something richer. Mrs Beeton recommended serving with apple sauce, sage and onion stuffing baked alongside, and roast potatoes cooked in the pork fat. Those accompaniments have changed very little. The crackling itself is eaten first, while it is still warm and crisp, broken into shards with the back of a spoon.
